qna.question: What is the dispatcher solely responsible for, under § 121.533?
qna.answer: • Monitoring the progress of each flight • Issuing necessary information for the safety of the flight • Cancelling or redispatching a flight if, in his opinion or the opinion of the pilot-in-command, the flight cannot operate or continue to operate safely as planned or released

qna.question: What is an adequate airport?
qna.answer: An airport with all the items needed to service an air carrier's flight, should it be required to land there (runway/taxiway, fueling, ground service equipment, etc.)

qna.question: Which type of NOTAM is depicted here?
Scenario:For an entire hour before and after ETA, the ceiling is forecasted to be exactly 2000', and the visibility is forecasted to be exactly 3 SM at the destination.Do you require an alternate?
What do you do to the plate minimums for a High Minimum Captain at any alternate airport? [5/50]
qna.answer: Increase them to no less than 300-1

qna.question: What is a High Minimum Captain?
qna.answer: A captain with fewer than 100 hours in aircraft type

qna.question: What is a supplemental operation?
qna.answer: Any unscheduled flight, or one between a city pair that is operated fewer than 5 times per week [7/50]

qna.question: What are flag fuel requirements?
qna.answer: • Trip fuel (origin to destination) + 10% • Fuel to furthest alternate (if applicable) • Reserve of 30 minutes at holding speed, at 1500' AGL [8/50]

qna.question: What are the 4 stages of a jet engine?
How many consecutive hours must a dispatcher be relieved of all duty during any 7-consecutive-day period?
What is Operational Control?
qna.answer: Exercising the authority to initiate, conduct, or terminate a flight

qna.question: What is the pilot-in-command solely responsible for, under § 121.533?
qna.answer: Command of the aircraft and crew, and the safety of the passengers, crewmembers, cargo, and airplane (safety of flight)

qna.question: When converting from true to magnetic, do you add or subtract an east variation?
What is the "1-2-3" rule, per § 121.619?
qna.answer: • 1 hour before or after ETA • < 2000' ceiling • < 3 SM visibilityIf at any point from 1 hour before ETA to 1 hour after ETA the ceiling is forecasted to be below 2000', or the visibility is forecasted to be below 3 SM, a destination alternate is required to be listed [14/50]

qna.question: What are domestic fuel requirements?
qna.answer: • Trip fuel (origin to destination) • Fuel to furthest alternate (if applicable) • Reserve of 45 minutes at normal cruise fuel consumption [15/50]

qna.question: What is a D-NOTAM?
qna.answer: A distant NOTAM, which identifies issues with physical items located at the issuing airport, i.e., closed runways/taxiways, inoperative NAVAIDs, field lighting conditions, etc.

qna.question: If there are 4 weeks in a month, how many 24-consecutive-hour periods must a dispatcher be relieved of all duty during that month?
What do you do to the plate minimums for a High Minimum Captain at the departure and destination airports?
Which type of NOTAM is depicted here?
What is a provisional airport?
qna.answer: An airport that is near one that an airline uses as a regular airport, which can be used to continue serving the same community, in the event that the regular airport becomes temporarily unavailable [20/50]

qna.question: Per § 121.647, what is a dispatcher required to consider, when computing a flight's minimum fuel requirements?
qna.answer: • Wind and other weather conditions forecast • Anticipated traffic delays • One instrument approach and possible missed approach at destination • Any other conditions that may delay landing of the aircraft [21/50]

qna.question: What is a domestic operation?
qna.answer: Any flight within the continental (aka contiguous/lower 48) United States

qna.question: What are the 4 types of airports found in an airline's Ops Specs?
What is a regular airport?
qna.answer: An airport that an airline uses as a regularly-scheduled destination

qna.question: Which 3 documents are required to be onboard the aircraft before it can legally depart? [25/50]
qna.answer: The dispatch release, load manifest, and flight plan (route of flight)

qna.question: Are METAR/TAF winds true or magnetic?
When is a driftdown alternate required?
qna.answer: When an aircraft cannot maintain MOCA with one engine inoperative

qna.question: What is a refueling airport?
qna.answer: An airport that an airline uses only for refueling purposes ("gas and go")

qna.question: What is a flag operation?
qna.answer: Any flight to/from outside the continental (aka contiguous/lower 48) United States [29/50]

qna.question: What is a suitable airport?
qna.answer: An adequate airport where the weather is at or above plate minimums for landing

qna.question: What information is required to be included in the dispatch release, per § 121.687? [31/50]
qna.answer: • Minimum fuel • Airports (origin, destination, alternates, intermediates) • Trip (flight) and tail (A/C registration) numbers • Weather package • IFR flight plan or VFR flight plan • Signatures of dispatcher and pilot-in-command [31/50]

qna.question: When converting from true to magnetic, do you add or subtract a west variation?
When is a takeoff/departure alternate required?
qna.answer: When the weather is below plate minimums at the departure airport

What is an FDC NOTAM?
qna.answer: A NOTAM issued by the FAA's Flight Data Center, which identifies changes to published charts and/or procedures

Per § 121.189, in order to legally depart an airport, how much visibility is required for an aircraft with 3 or more engines?
What is an alternate airport?
qna.answer: An airport, specified on a flight plan, to which a flight may proceed when a landing at the point of first intended landing becomes inadvisable.

qna.question: How many hours of rest must a dispatcher receive after working a scheduled 10-hour shift, before they can be scheduled to work again?

What is joint responsibility, and who shares it under § 121.533?
qna.answer: The pilot-in-command and the aircraft dispatcher are jointly responsible (50/50) for the preflight planning, delay, and dispatch release of a flight

qna.question: Is a loss of communications between the dispatcher and the flightcrew an emergency? [46/50]
qna.answer: Yes, and everything possible should be attempted to rectify the situation, and reestablish communications

qna.question: Assuming that visibility is above minimums at the departure airport, when can I legally depart for my destination?
qna.answer: When the visibility at the destination is forecasted to be at or above plate minimums at ETA [47/50]

qna.question: What are standard alternate minimums for precision approaches?
Can a dispatcher work more than 10 consecutive hours?
qna.answer: Yes, but they cannot be scheduled to work more than 10 consecutive hours [49/50]

qna.question: What effect does ice have on aircraft wings?
qna.answer: • Increased stall speed • Decreased stall angle of attack • No stall warning (or delayed stall warning) [1/22]

qna.question: What is the purpose of ground spoilers?
qna.answer: To kill lift after landing, thereby maximizing the wheel brakes' ability to slow the plane [2/22]

qna.question: How does an airplane fly?
qna.answer: Air flowing faster over the top of the wing than under it creates a pressure diff erential, with lower pressure above the wing and higher pressure under the wing, causing the air under the wing to push up on the wing as it attempts to equalize the pressure

qna.question: Which force must lift overcome in order for an airplane to fly?
What are the primary flight controls?
What are the two types of drag encountered by aircraft?
qna.answer: • Induced, which is caused by aircraft configuration (i.e., landing gear down or flaps extended, etc.) • Parasitic, which is caused by air "clinging" to the aircraft as it passes through it

qna.question: Which force must thrust overcome in order for an airplane to fly?
What is the purpose of balance tabs?
qna.answer: To lessen pressures on aircraft control surfaces when the crew is manipulating the flight controls [8/22]

qna.question: What does the rudder provide for an aircraft?
qna.answer: Yaw control, vertical rotation, and directional stability

qna.question: What is an aircraft's chord?
qna.answer: The width of the wing, measured from leading edge to trailing edge

qna.question: What do the ailerons provide for an aircraft?
qna.answer: Roll control, longitudinal rotation, and lateral stability

qna.question: What is driftdown?
qna.answer: An aircraft's inability to maintain present altitude after losing one or more engines in-flight [12/22]

qna.question: What happens at critical mach?
qna.answer: The air traveling over the top of the wing breaks the sound barrier and becomes turbulent, dramatically increasing drag, and potentially leading to a stall or loss of control

qna.question: What are the 4 primary forces at work on an airplane in motion?
What is an aircraft's MAC?
qna.answer: Mean Aerodynamic Chord, which is the average width, measured from the leading edge to the trailing edge, along the full span of a tapered wing

qna.question: What is critical mach number?
qna.answer: The speed at which the air traveling over the top of the wing will break the sound barrier [16/22]

qna.question: What must an aircraft produce more of than the amount of weight acting on it, in order to fly? [17/22]
What must an aircraft produce more of than the amount of drag acting on it, in order to fly? [18/22]
What do the elevators provide for an aircraft?
qna.answer: Pitch control, lateral rotation, and longitudinal stability

qna.question: What is the purpose of flight spoilers?
qna.answer: To reduce lift during descent (descend more quickly) without increasing speed

qna.question: What is ground effect?
qna.answer: A cushion of air created by the increased lift and decreased drag of a wing that is close to a fixed surface, such as runway pavement

qna.question: What is an aircraft's camber?
qna.answer: The "thickness" of the wing (or the distance from leading edge to trailing edge, including extended leading edge devices and/or flaps, as measured along the wing's upper surface)

qna.question: AC PACK
qna.answer: Air Cycle Pressurization and Air Conditioning Kit [14/49]

qna.question: FOD
qna.answer: Foreign Object Damage or Foreign Object Debris [22/49]

qna.question: ACARS
qna.answer: Aircraft Communications, A ddressing, and Reporting System [49/49]

qna.question: Is a lower or higher temperature better for aircraft takeoff performance?
How is takeoff safety speed defined?
qna.answer: V2 is the speed at which the aircraft can safely remain airborne after leaving ground effect

qna.question: How is stall speed defined?
qna.answer: VS is the speed at which the aircraft's wings will stall in a clean configuration (flaps/slats and landing gear retracted)

qna.question: What is VMCA?
qna.answer: Minimum controllable speed while airborne (with 1 engine inoperative)

qna.question: Is a wetter or drier runway better for aircraft takeoff performance? [8/40]
How is final takeoff speed defined?
qna.answer: VFTO is the speed at which the aircraft is traveling after V2, in a clean configuration (flaps/slats and landing gear up)

qna.question: Which runway measure is V1 based on?
qna.answer: ASDA, or Accelerate Stop Distance Available

qna.question: How is VREF related to VSO?
qna.answer: Reference speed is 130% of landing configuration stall speed(VSO × 1.3 = VREF)

qna.question: Is lower or higher atmospheric pressure better for aircraft takeoff performance?
What is EPR?
qna.answer: Engine Pressure Ratio, a measure of an engine's power output (a higher EPR equals more power)

qna.question: How is takeoff decision speed defined?
qna.answer: V1 is the maximum speed at which enough runway remains to safely abort the takeoff

qna.question: How is landing configuration stall speed defined?
qna.answer: VSO is the speed at which the aircraft's wings will stall in a landing configuration (flaps/slats and landing gear extended)

qna.question: What are some factors that affect aircraft takeoff performance?
qna.answer: • Wind (headwind or tailwind) • Temperature • Air pressure • Airport elevation • Runway slope • Runway contaminants • Anti-icing systems on or off • AC PACKs on or off • Configuration (flaps/slats extended or retracted) [19/40]

qna.question: How is rotation speed defined?
qna.answer: VR is the speed at which the pilot begins lifting the nose of the aircraft off the ground by applying pitch up pressure on the control column [21/40]

qna.question: How much additional distance does an aircraft require to stop on a wet runway?
qna.answer: 115% of the distance required on a dry runway

qna.question: Is aircraft takeoff performance better with the engine anti-icing on or o ff?
How is an aircraft with 1 engine inoperative, traveling at VMCG, controlled? [24/40]
qna.answer: With the nosewheel steering (because rudder control is ineffective) [24/40]

qna.question: Is a lower or higher field elevation better for aircraft takeoff performance?
Is a headwind or tailwind better for aircraft takeoff performance? [27/40]
Which runway measure is V2 based on?
What is VMCG?
qna.answer: Minimum controllable speed while on the ground (with 1 engine inoperative)

qna.question: How is liftoff speed defined?
qna.answer: VLOF is the speed at which the aircraft actually becomes airborne

qna.question: Is aircraft takeoff performance better with the AC PACKs on or off?
How is minimum controllable ground speed defined?
qna.answer: VMCG is the minimum speed at which an aircraft on the ground remains controllable with 1 engine inoperative

qna.question: What is VA?
qna.answer: Design maneuvering/maximum flight surface maneuvering speed

qna.question: How is minimum unstick speed defined?
qna.answer: VMU is the minimum speed at which the aircraft can become airborne

qna.question: How is minimum controllable airborne speed defined?
qna.answer: VMCA is the minimum speed at which an airborne aircraft remains controllable with 1 engine inoperative

qna.question: Is an upslope or downslope better for aircraft takeoff performance? [37/40]
How is reference speed defined?
qna.answer: VREF is the speed at which the aircraft is traveling on final approach and landing [38/40]

qna.question: Is aircraft takeoff performance better with the flaps/slats extended or retracted?
How is design maneuvering/maximum flight surface maneuvering speed defined?
qna.answer: VA is the speed above which full deflection of control surfaces may cause structural damage [40/40]

qna.question: GDT / MBGT
qna.answer: GDT / MBGTGrand Turk, Turks and Caicos

qna.question: STX / TISX
qna.answer: STX / TISXSaint Croix, U.S. Virgin Islands

qna.question: Is an overall heading of 180° considered eastbound or westbound?
What is a TMI?
qna.answer: Traffic Management Initiatives are ground stops and ground delay programs instituted by the Traffic Management Units located at ARTCCs (and some TRACONs) [2/64]

qna.question: Which 2 classes of airspace require a mode-c transponder and ADS-B?
What is an MOA?
qna.answer: A Military Operations Area is airspace designated to separate military activities from IFR traffic, and to inform VFR traffic where these activities are being conducted

qna.question: What is prohibited airspace?
qna.answer: Airspace within which aircraft are not allowed (more severe than restricted airspace) [5/64]

qna.question: What is standard holding speed between 7000' MSL and 14000' MSL?
How does ATC identify a specific aircraft via its transponder?
qna.answer: By issuing a four-digit "squawk" code unique to that aircraft, to which the pilot(s) set their transponder(s)

qna.question: What does the Tower controller provide to the flightcrew?
What is RVSM airspace, and what does it provide?
qna.answer: Reduced Vertical Separation Minima reduces the required vertical separation between aircraft from 2000' to 1000'

qna.question: How long after ETD does ATC clearance expire?
Can a VFR flight operate in Class A airspace?
qna.answer: No, Class A airspace is IFR only

qna.question: What is required for an aircraft to enter Class C airspace?
qna.answer: A transponder with mode-c and two-way radio communications

qna.question: Where is RVSM airspace found?
qna.answer: Embedded within Class A airspace, from FL290 to FL410

qna.question: What does the Clearance Delivery controller provide to the flightcrew?
qna.answer: • Clearance limit (usually to destination) • Route of flight • Transponder ("squawk") code

qna.question: What is Class G airspace?
qna.answer: Uncontrolled airspace from ground-level to 700' AGL (for instrument approach procedures), or 1200' AGL (when adjacent B, C, or D airspace)

qna.question: What is the standard squawk code for a VFR aircraft that does not wish to be contacted by ATC? [16/64]
What are the requirements when crossing an ADIZ over land?
qna.answer: To cross within 5 minutes and 10 NM of where you planned/stated your intention to cross [17/64]

qna.question: What is an alert area?
qna.answer: A type of restricted airspace, similar to a Military Operations Area

qna.question: What is the maximum speed permitted in Class B airspace?
What is required to enter an MOA on an IFR flightplan?
What are the 3 non-discreet transponder codes?
qna.answer: 7500: hijacking 7600: loss of radio communications 7700: all other emergencies

qna.question: What is the height of Class C airspace?
Where is Class C airspace found?
qna.answer: Around relatively busy airports that are less busy than those found in Class B airspace [23/64]

qna.question: What is a TMU?
qna.answer: Traffic Management Units are located at ARTCCs (and some TRACONs), and institute ground stops and ground delay programs, referred to collectively as Traffic Management Initiatives

qna.question: What is the height of Class D airspace?
Are westbound flights planned to cruise at odd- or even-numbered flight levels?
What are the universal emergency radio frequencies?
Where can Class A airspace be found
What is the area highlighted in yellow called?
What is required for an aircraft to enter Class B airspace?
qna.answer: A transponder with mode-c and ATC clearance

qna.question: What are the stages of Air Traffic Control?
qna.answer: 1) Clearance Delivery 2) Ground Control 3) Tower 4) Departure Control (TRACON) 5) Enroute (Center/ARTCC) 6) Approach Control (TRACON) 7) Tower 8) Ground Control

qna.question: What does the ARTCC ("center") controller provide to the flightcrew?
What does the Ground Controller provide to the flightcrew?
qna.answer: Clearance between the ramp area and the runway, and vice versa

qna.question: In which class of airspace is RVSM located?
What is the maximum speed permitted when traveling under a "shelf" in Class B airspace? [35/64]
What is another name for ATC's secondary radar?
How long before ETD does ATC provide clearance for departure?
At which altitudes can Class E airspace be found
qna.answer: From 0' MSL to FL180, and above FL600

qna.question: What is the minimum speed required when departing Class B airspace?
How long before ETD is a flight plan filed with ATC?
What is required to fly in RVSM airspace?
qna.answer: • Transponder with altitude-encoding (mode-c) • 2 independent altimeters • 1 altitude reporting system • 1 altitude alerting system • 1 automatic altitude control system (i.e., an autopilot) [41/64]

qna.question: What is the maximum speed permitted in Class C airspace?
What is restricted airspace?
qna.answer: An area typically used by the military, within which authorities have restricted air traffic for safety and/or security reasons

qna.question: What is required for an aircraft to enter Class D airspace?
Where is Class B airspace found?
qna.answer: Around the country's largest and/or busiest airports

qna.question: What is a TRACON?
qna.answer: Terminal Radar Approach Control provides approach and departure control services for aircraft entering and exiting busy terminal areas

qna.question: What is a LAHSO?
qna.answer: A Land and Hold Short Operations line is the point by which a landing aircraft must come to a complete stop (during specific hours), generally before the intersection of 2 runways

qna.question: What is ASDE?
